title: Visible-NIR-Frequency Hyperbolic Response in Nodal-Line Semimetal PbTaSe$_2$
authors: Vivian J. Santamaria-Garcia, Morgan G. Blevins, Simo Pajovic, Carolina Orona-Navar, Svetlana V. Boriskina
abstract: Natural hyperbolic materials offer a powerful platform for light-matter interactions by supporting highly anisotropic electromagnetic modes without the need for artificial patterning. In this work, we experimentally demonstrate that the nodal-line semimetal PbTaSe$_2$ exhibits robust hyperbolic optical behavior in the visible to near-infrared spectral range, which arises intrinsically from its anisotropic electronic structure and layered crystal symmetry. By combining first-principles calculations, ellipsometry, Drude-Lorentz modeling, and reflectance measurements, we establish a consistent experimental and theoretical picture of bulk hyperbolicity in this material. This hyperbolicity is of plasmonic origin and is characterized by a competitive quality factor ($Q_\mathrm{max} \approx 2.8$) and a very large anisotropy parameter ($|R| \approx 231$) at 0.78 eV.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the nodal-line semimetal PbTaSe2 acts as a natural hyperbolic material in the visible and near-infrared spectrum. Unlike many known hyperbolic materials that operate in the mid-IR, this material provides a platform for high-anisotropy light-matter interaction at higher frequencies. This discovery is significant for developing unpatterned, electronic-based hyperbolic nanophotonics.
main_problem: The study aims to characterize the intrinsic hyperbolic optical response of the nodal-line semimetal PbTaSe2 in the visible to near-infrared spectral range and identify the spectral windows for Type-II hyperbolic dispersion.
main_result: The researchers experimentally demonstrated that PbTaSe2 exhibits robust Type-II hyperbolic behavior in the visible-NIR range, characterized by a large anisotropy parameter (|R| approx 231) and a competitive quality factor (Qmax approx 2.8).
method: The work combines experimental spectroscopic ellipsometry, AFM, and reflection spectroscopy with first-principles DFT calculations and Drude-Lorentz modeling of the dielectric tensor.
model_or_system: The physical system is PbTaSe2, a layered, non-centrosymmetric, nodal-line topological semimetal with a hexagonal crystal structure. The optical model uses a uniaxial dielectric tensor (epsilon_ab and epsilon_c) and a multilayer stack of PbTaSe2 on SiO2/Si.
key_observables: In-plane and out-of-plane dielectric functions (epsilon_ab, epsilon_c), optical conductivity, anisotropy parameter (|R|), and hyperbolic quality factor (Q).
important_parameters: Type-II hyperbolic regimes between 0.8-1.4 eV and 1.9-2.6 eV; anisotropy parameter |R| approx 231 at 0.78 eV; quality factor Qmax approx 2.8.
main_assumptions: The crystallographic c-axis is assumed to be parallel to the substrate normal; an isotropic Drude-Lorentz model is used as an initial baseline for the optical response.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Crystal structure, SOC-induced Fermi surface, and orbital-projected band structure. Fig 2: Experimental ellipsometry data, DL model fits, and extracted dielectric functions/conductivity. Fig 3: Identification of Type-II hyperbolic regimes in experimental and DFT data.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of natural hyperbolic materials, describes the material properties and experimental setup, presents the spectroscopic ellipsometry and DFT results, identifies the hyperbolic spectral windows, and concludes with the significance of the findings for nanophotonics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Electronic structure of PbTaSe2 and spin–orbit coupling effects (A) Crystal structure of PbTaSe2, highlighting its layered hexagonal symmetry and the inequivalent in-plane (ab) and out-of-plane (c-axis) directions that enable anisotropic electronic dispersion and optical response. (B) Fermi surface calculated without (top) and with (bottom) SOC, showing substantial SOC-induced reconstruction of low-energy elec- tronic states near the Fermi level, including lifted degeneracies and reshaped pockets associated with nodal- line topology. (C) Orbital-projected band structure and density of states, indicating dominant Ta d-orbital character near the Fermi level. (D) SOC-induced band...

Figure 2: Uniaxial anisotropic dielectric response of PbTaSe2 (A) A micrograph of the transferred flake and a schematic of the multi-layer structure used for fitting ellipsometric data. (B) Ellipsometric angle Ψ measured at angles of incidence of 40°, 50°, and 60°, fitted using an anisotropic DL model consisting of two Lorentz oscillators and one Drude term for εab and three Lorentz oscillators and one Drude term for εc (Table 1). (C) Corresponding spectra of ellipsometric angle ∆at the same angles of incidence, showing good agreement with the optical model. (D,E) Real and imaginary parts of the in-plane dielectric function, εab, extracted from the experimental data using a uniaxial...

Figure 3: Identification of hyperbolic optical regimes in PbTaSe2. (A) Real parts of the in-plane and out-of- plane dielectric functions from anisotropic DL modeling of experimental data. The shaded regions around the fit represent the 95 % confidence intervals (CI) (B) Corresponding real parts from first-principles calculations. The vertical shaded regions indicate spectral regions with opposite signs of εab and εc, where PbTaSe2 has Type-II hyperbolic dispersion.

Figure 4: Spectral regions and performance of natural uniaxial hyperbolic van der Waals materials. (A) Type-I and Type-II hyperbolic spectral regions as predicted by first-principles calculations (green and blue) and as measured experimentally (yellow and red). (B) Maximum hyperbolic quality factor Q as predicted by first-principles calculations vs. experimental measurements (Table S2) [10, 24, 31–38, 40]. (C) Maximum hyperbolic quality factor Qmax as a function of energy considering only experimental reports where the hyperbolic response is attributed to or strongly associated with plasmonic behavior, emphasizing the trade- off between operating energy and optical performance.
